title-group.article-title: Diagnostic accuracy and feasibility of artificial intelligence-driven smartphone imaging for dental caries detection: A systematic review☆

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of International Collaborative and Innovative Dentistry, Graduate School of Dentistry, Tohoku University, Sendai, Miyagi, Japan
bInternational Graduate Program in Dental Public Health, Department of Community Dentistry, Faculty of Dentistry, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok, Thailand
cSchool of Dentistry, University of Baguio, Baguio City, Philippines
dDepartment of Orthodontics, Faculty of Dental Medicine, Universitas Airlangga, Surabaya, East Java, Indonesia
eDepartment of Community Dentistry, Faculty of Dentistry, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok, Thailand

author-notes.corresp: ⁎Correspondence to: Department of International Collaborative and Innovative Dentistry, Graduate School of Dentistry, Tohoku University, 4-1 Seiryo-machi, Aoba-ku, Sendai, Miyagi 980-8575, Japan. hong.guang.d6@tohoku.ac.jp

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2025 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The Japanese Association for Dental Science.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: This systematic review assesses the diagnostic accuracy, feasibility, and clinical performance of artificial intelligence (AI)-based smartphone imaging tools for detecting dental caries.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Following the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses for Diagnostic Test Accuracy (PRISMA-DTA) guidelines, five databases: PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, Embase, and Cochrane Library, were searched up to March 26, 2025. This study was registered with the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO) (CRD420251047689). Diagnostic accuracy and feasibility of AI-driven analysis of smartphone-based dental images for the detection of dental caries were assessed. Risk of bias and applicability were evaluated using QUADAS-2.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Fourteen studies met the inclusion criteria. AI models, particularly YOLO variants, DenseNet201, and MobileNetV3, demonstrated high diagnostic accuracy, especially for cavitated lesions, with some outperforming junior dentists. Enhanced YOLO models achieved up to 85.5 % mean average precision. Tools were generally user-friendly and suitable for community or at-home screening. However, sensitivity for early or non-cavitated lesions varied.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: AI-driven smartphone imaging shows promise as an accessible and reliable tool for caries detection, particularly in low-resource or remote settings. Further research is needed to improve early lesion detection, ensure clinical validation, and support equitable implementation.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Dental caries remains one of the most prevalent oral health issues globally 1, 2, 3, affecting individuals of all ages, particularly children 4, 5 and the elderly 6, 7. Despite advancements in dental care, early detection and timely intervention remain challenging, especially in underserved populations 8, 9 where access to professional dental services is limited. Delayed diagnosis and inadequate treatment planning contribute to disease progression, increasing the burden on healthcare systems and negatively impacting overall well-being and quality of life 10, 11, 12, 13.

Conventional caries detection methods, including visual-tactile examination and radiographic imaging, have been the gold standard for decades. However, these methods are often subjective and may lack sensitivity in detecting early-stage lesions or caries in difficult-to-visualize areas [14]. Emerging advanced diagnostic technologies, such as fluorescence-based imaging and optical coherence tomography, have demonstrated improved sensitivity and specificity in detecting early carious lesions 3, 15. However, many dental professionals continue to rely on traditional methods, leading to missed diagnoses and delayed interventions [15]. There is a growing need for standardized diagnostic criteria and protocols to ensure consistent and accurate detection of early carious lesions across various clinical settings.

Artificial intelligence (AI) is revolutionizing healthcare by enhancing diagnostic accuracy, decision-making, and personalized treatment strategies [16]. In dentistry, AI-driven tools, including machine learning algorithms and deep learning-based image analysis, are increasingly being explored for dental caries detection 17, 18, 19. While AI-assisted radiographic analysis has demonstrated good diagnostic accuracy and strong potential for clinical use 20, 21, 22, smartphone-based imaging combined with AI provides a cost-effective, fast, and user-friendly solution, with the potential to bring advanced diagnostic capabilities directly into the hands of dentists worldwide 23, 24, 25.

Applications accessible via a smartphone have the potential to enhance access to oral healthcare in underserved and resource-constrained areas, supporting automated diagnoses and advancing teledentistry applications 26, 27. The integration of AI with smartphone technology enables early detection and intervention in early childhood caries (ECC), offering significant benefits for preventive and pediatric dentistry. Its large-scale use could improve children's oral health and help reduce the overall burden of dental caries on healthcare systems [28]. A smartphone-based system for real-time early childhood caries diagnosis to fill in a major gap in ECC diagnosis and prevention in the current healthcare system [29]. Embracing these technological advancements in healthcare can lead to improved patient outcomes, telemedicine applications, and a broader positive impact on oral cancer management globally, ushering in a new era of early detection and treatment.

Despite the potential of AI-assisted smartphone imaging in dental caries detection, there are some limitations. Available research on AI-based smartphone imaging in dentistry is still limited, with most studies focusing on oral photographs or radiographic AI analysis rather than direct smartphone-based intraoral imaging. Thus, there is a critical need for a systematic synthesis of the current evidence to evaluate diagnostic accuracy, feasibility, and clinical applicability of these emerging technologies. The findings of this systematic review provide evidence on AI-assisted smartphone imaging as a tool for caries detection and provide valuable information on the potential of AI-driven smartphone imaging to improve dental caries management. AI-based smartphone tools could play a transformative role in modern dentistry, telehealth, and public health strategies for oral disease prevention by improving diagnostic precision and increasing accessibility. This review highlights public health applications, particularly for remote and underserved populations, and may guide AI model development and clinical integration.

This systematic review aims to evaluate the current literature on AI-driven smartphone imaging for dental caries detection and diagnosis. Specifically, it: 1) assesses the diagnostic accuracy (sensitivity, specificity, predictive values) of AI-driven smartphone imaging compared to conventional methods; 2) evaluates the feasibility and usability of AI-driven smartphone imaging in clinical and public health settings; and 3) identifies barriers and facilitators for integrating AI-powered smartphone imaging into routine dental practice.

sec.title: Protocol and registration
sec.p:
This systematic review was conducted according to the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses for Diagnostic Test Accuracy (PRISMA-DTA) guidelines [30]. The study protocol was registered on the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO) database under the registration number CRD420251047689. The focused question was addressed using the participant, intervention, comparison, and outcomes (PICO) criteria (Table 1).Table 1
Description of the PICO (P = Participant, I = Intervention, C = Comparison, O = Outcome) elements.Table 1
Research QuestionWhat is the diagnostic accuracy and feasibility of AI-driven analysis of smartphone-based dental images for dental caries detection compared to conventional diagnostic methods in individuals undergoing dental caries assessment?
ParticipantIndividuals of any age undergoing dental caries assessment using smartphone-based imaging technologies.
InterventionAI-driven analysis of smartphone dental images for caries detection.
ComparisonConventional diagnostic methods such as visual-tactile examination, radiography, or fluorescence-based detection.
OutcomeDiagnostic accuracy (e.g., sensitivity, specificity, positive/negative predictive values) and feasibility (e.g., clinical usability, image acquisition protocols, integration potential).

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p:
Inclusion Criteria:
Original, full-text, open-access articles available through Tohoku University Graduate School of Dentistry IP access.
Studies evaluating AI-based analysis of smartphone-captured dental images for caries detection.
Accepted study designs: randomized controlled trials (RCTs), cohort studies, cross-sectional studies, diagnostic accuracy studies, and comparative studies with conventional diagnostic methods.
Studies reporting relevant data on diagnostic accuracy and/or feasibility.

Exclusion Criteria:
Studies that focus exclusively on AI analysis of non-smartphone-based imaging (e.g., radiographs, intraoral cameras not attached to smartphones).
Studies using smartphone images without AI involvement (i.e., solely for visual/manual analysis).
Articles not published in English.
Case reports, case series, expert opinions, conference papers, review articles, editorials, and short communications.
Studies lacking sufficient data on diagnostic accuracy or feasibility outcomes.

sec.title: Information sources and search strategies
sec.p:
Electronic databases such as PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, Embase, and Cochrane Library were searched for relevant articles that were published on March 26, 2025. Besides the electronic databases, reference lists of the selected studies were manually screened. Table 2 shows the search strategies in the selected databases.Table 2
Databases and search strategies of this study.Table 2
DatabaseSearch Strategy
PubMed(Artificial intelligence OR AI OR machine learning OR deep learning OR neural networks) AND (smartphone OR smartphone application OR mobile OR mobile application OR mobile health OR teledentistry) AND (dental caries OR tooth decay OR caries OR caries detection OR caries assessment OR caries diagnosis)
Scopus(“Artificial intelligence” OR “AI” OR “machine learning” OR “deep learning” OR “neural networks”) AND (“smartphone” OR “smartphone application” OR “mobile” OR “mobile application” OR “mobile health” OR “teledentistry”) AND (“dental caries” OR “tooth decay” OR “caries” OR “caries detection” OR “caries assessment” OR “caries diagnosis”)
Web of ScienceTS= ("Artificial intelligence" OR "AI" OR "machine learning" OR "deep learning" OR "neural networks")AND TS= ("smartphone" OR "smartphone application" OR "mobile" OR "mobile application" OR "mobile health" OR "teledentistry")AND TS= ("dental caries" OR "tooth decay" OR "caries" OR "caries detection" OR "caries assessment" OR "caries diagnose*")
EMBASE('artificial intelligence'/exp OR 'artificial intelligence':ti,ab OR 'ai':ti,ab OR 'machine learning':ti,ab OR 'deep learning':ti,ab OR 'neural networks':ti,ab) AND ('smartphone'/exp OR 'smartphone':ti,ab OR 'smartphone application':ti,ab OR 'mobile':ti,ab OR 'mobile application':ti,ab OR 'mobile health':ti,ab OR 'teledentistry':ti,ab) AND ('dental caries'/exp OR 'dental caries':ti,ab OR 'tooth decay':ti,ab OR 'caries':ti,ab OR 'caries detection':ti,ab OR 'caries assessment':ti,ab OR 'caries diagnose*':ti,ab)
Cochrane LibraryArtificial intelligence OR AI OR machine learning OR deep learning OR neural networks AND smartphone OR smartphone application OR mobile OR mobile application OR mobile health OR teledentistry AND dental caries OR tooth decay OR caries OR caries detection OR caries assessment OR caries diagnosis in Title AbstractKeyword-in Trials, Clinical Answers

sec.title: Study selection and data extraction
sec.p: A two-stage screening process was conducted. In the first stage, two reviewers (J.M.A. and P.P.P.) independently screened titles and abstracts for relevance. In the second stage, full-text articles of potentially eligible studies were assessed based on the predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. Disagreements were resolved through discussion or consultation with a third reviewer (J.R.V.S., G.H.). All references were managed using EndNote version 21.0 (Clarivate Analytics, US), and duplicates were removed prior to screening.

Using a predesigned extraction form, the following information was extracted by the two authors independently (J.M.A. and A.P.N.) from the papers that met the eligibility criteria: title, authors’ names, year of publication, country, objective of the study, study design, type of dentition, dataset or modality, AI platform, and main findings. Extraction followed the PICO framework: participants (P): individuals undergoing dental caries assessment; intervention (I): AI model and smartphone imaging protocol; comparison (C): conventional diagnostic methods (visual-tactile examination, radiography, or fluorescence-based detection); and outcomes (O): diagnostic performance (sensitivity, specificity, area under the Receiver Operating Characteristics curve) and feasibility and usability metrics (user-friendliness, reliability).

sec.title: Risk-of-bias assessment
sec.p: The quality assessment of the included papers was conducted by two independent authors (J.M.A. and A.P.N.) using the Quality Assessment of Diagnostic Accuracy Studies (QUADAS-2) tool [31]. The QUADAS-2 tool evaluates four key domains that may introduce bias in diagnostic accuracy studies: patient selection, index test, reference standard, and flow and timing. Each domain includes signaling questions that guide the assessment. If all signaling questions within a domain were answered "yes," the risk of bias for that domain was judged as low. If any signaling question were answered "no," the risk was considered high. The risk of bias was recorded as “unclear” only when insufficient data were presented.

sec.title: Diagnostic accuracy measures
sec.p: To evaluate and synthesize the diagnostic performance of AI-based caries detection tools, we employed a structured approach that accounted for methodological variability across studies. The following strategies that addressed heterogeneity in test designs, AI models, datasets, reference standards, and outcome definitions, were applied:
a) Handling of multiple definitions of the target condition

The included studies used various ways to define dental caries, including clinical visual-tactile criteria (e.g., modified ICDAS), radiographic evidence, and diagnostic consensus. We grouped studies by how they defined caries based on caries severity (e.g., cavitated vs. non-cavitated lesions, primary vs. secondary caries) and reported diagnostic outcomes accordingly. Studies were grouped to allow comparison across similar definitions.
b) Handling of multiple thresholds of test positivity

Several AI models used differing confidence or decision thresholds (e.g., Intersection over Union (IoU), softmax probabilities, mAP cutoffs) to classify carious lesions. When studies reported performance metrics at multiple thresholds or model enhancements (e.g., standard YOLOv3 vs. enhanced YOLOv3), each configuration was treated as a distinct data point. Reported sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, and F1 scores were synthesized accordingly.
c) Handling of multiple index test readers

Although AI systems function independently, some studies incorporated comparisons with human examiners, such as general dentists, specialists, or practitioners with different experience levels. When available, these comparisons were included to provide context, with attention to the training and calibration of human readers.
d) Handling of indeterminate test results

Most studies did not explicitly report the handling of indeterminate or unclear results. However, in studies involving user-captured images (e.g., parents using smartphones), low image quality occasionally led to uncertain AI predictions. These were described narratively, though no statistical adjustments were made due to limited data.
e) Grouping and comparing tests
To facilitate meaningful comparison, AI models were grouped according to:

Detection: YOLO variants—including YOLOv3, YOLOv4, YOLOv5, YOLOv5s, YOLOv5x, YOLO (v5, v5n, v5s, v5m, v51), Faster Region-Based Convolutional Neural Networks (Faster R-CNNs) and Single-Shot Multi-Box Detector (SSD).

Classification: Deep learning architectures such as ResNet (ResNet-18, ResNet-50, ResNet-101, ResNet-50V2, ResNet-101V2, ResNet-152) and DenseNet (DenseNet-169, DenseNet-201), VGG16, AlexNet, Support Vector Machine (SVM), GoogleNet, Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), and a 2D–3D Hybrid CNN with Efficient Channel Attention (ECA).
Segmentation: MobileNetV3.

Diagnostic metrics were synthesized within and across model groups to identify relative performance. When studies evaluated more than one model on the same dataset, their results were especially valuable for direct comparison.
f) Handling of different reference standards

Reference standards varied across studies and included clinical visual-tactile examinations (often based on ICDAS or similar criteria), radiographic assessments, expert consensus, and in vitro validation with extracted teeth. Outcomes were stratified based on reference standard type to assess the influence of the comparator on reported accuracy.

sec.title: Synthesis of results
sec.p: Due to the heterogeneity in AI architectures, datasets, image modalities, study populations, and diagnostic outcomes, a meta-analysis was not feasible. Instead, we conducted a narrative synthesis of findings. Performance metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, F1 score, and mean average precision (mAP) were extracted from each study and summarized.

To further describe and interpret the variability observed: studies were grouped by the type of AI model the source of images (e.g., smartphone photographs, extracted teeth), and clinical application (e.g., real-time chairside detection vs. retrospective image analysis); major sources of heterogeneity included differences in dataset size, augmentation techniques, image quality, patient age group (children vs. adults), and dentition type; and subgroup patterns were noted.

Our synthesis highlights how AI tools can vary widely in performance depending on context, but also reveals their potential in both clinical and community-based applications. While direct comparison across all studies was limited, this approach allowed for meaningful insights into the current landscape of AI-driven caries detection. Furthermore, the illustration of AI-driven smartphone imaging for dental caries detection is shown in Fig. 1.Fig. 1
PRISMA flowchart for the search process.Fig. 1

sec.title: Study selection and search results
sec.p:
Initially, 146 articles were identified. After 54 duplicate articles were removed, 92 articles underwent title and abstract screening, 49 articles failed to meet the inclusion criteria, 2 articles were not retrieved because they do not have the full text article, and 27 articles were excluded following full-text assessment. This resulted in 14 articles in the final analysis. Fig. 2 depicts the flow diagram of the study selection process.Fig. 2
Illustration of artificial intelligence-driven smartphone imaging for dental caries detection.Fig. 2

sec.title: Study characteristics
sec.p:
The characteristics of the included studies are summarized chronologically in Table 3. The publication dates ranged from 2021 to 2025. Studies have been conducted across various countries, including China, Vietnam, the USA, India, Bangladesh, Pakistan, Indonesia, and Australia. Most of the studies aimed to develop, validate, or evaluate AI-based systems. Across the included studies, AI platforms varied according to task type. For detection, YOLO models including YOLOv3, YOLOv4, YOLOv5, YOLOv5s, YOLOv5x, YOLO (v5, v5n, v5s, v5m, v51 were most common appearing in seven studies 32, 33, 34, 35, 36, 37, 38, alongside Faster Region-Based Convolutional Neural Networks (Faster R-CNNs) and Single-Shot Multi-Box Detector (SSD) [38]. For classification, 2 studies used ResNet variants (ResNet-18, ResNet50, ResNet101, ResNet50V2, ResNet101V2, ResNet152), DenseNet variants (DenseNet169, DenseNet201), VGG16, and AlexNet 37, 39, while others employed Support Vector Machine (SVM) [40], Google Net [41], standard Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) [42], and a 2-D 3-D Hybrid Convolutional Neural Network with Efficient Channel Attention (ECA) [43]. One study employed MobileNetV3 [44], which was adapted for segmentation tasks. One study did not specify the type of AI platform that was used [45].Table 3
Characteristics of the included studies using artificial intelligence to detect dental caries with smartphones.Table 3
Authors, YearCountryObjective of the StudyStudy DesignType of DentitionDataset / ModalityLabelling procedureAnnotation toolImage preprocessing AugmentationTask UndertakenObject/ TargetAI Platform/ ModelEvaluation MetricsResults (%)
Ding et al., 2021 [32]ChinaDetect dental cavities using YOLOv3 on mobile photosDiagnostic performance evaluationPrimary & permanent3 datasets: 3990 each (augmented, enhanced), 7980 combined from 570 smartphone imagesSpecialist cliniciansLabelImg softwareData augmentationImage enhancement using Multi scale retinex with color restoration (MSRCR)ObjectDetectionDental caries lesionsYOLOv3Mean average precision (mAP)Precision Recall (Sensitivity) F1-score (Primary)85.4893.3369.4280
Duong et al., 2021 [40]VietnamIdentify occlusal lesions via smartphone using ICDASExperimentalN/A620 extracted teeth photographed using smartphone4 dentistsManual (Tool not specified)Preprocessing using OpenCV: Grayscale + blur, binary conversion, erode/dilate, find contour, make mask, fill background & restore imageNo data augmentationClassificationDental caries status of occlusal surfaceSVMAccuracy SensitivitySpecificity92.388.196.6
Duong et al., 2021 [41]VietnamDetect/classify caries via smartphoneProof-of-conceptN/A587 extracted tooth images using smartphone4 dentistsManual (Tool not specified)Image enhance module & histogram equalization, Grayscale & binary conversion, contour detection and masking using OpenCVObject detection & ClassificationDental caries lesionsResNet, GoogleNetAccuracySensitivitySpecificity87.3989.8868.86
Al-Jallad et al., 2022 [45]USAEvaluate app usability for ECC detectionMixed methods (usability test)Primary & permanent37 photos of children’s teeth taken by parent’s smartphoneA trained dentistNot reportedImage Quality Checker that assesses the blurriness, darkness, and brightness of the imageObject DetectionDental caries lesionsNot specifiedSUS score78.4
Priya et al., 2022 [42]IndiaBuild deep learning model in mobile appDevelopmental & validationN/A1500 tooth images from KaggleN/ANot reportedImage resizing, rescaling, & augmentationClassificationDental caries vs. non-cariesCNNAccuracy96
Thanh et al., 2022 [38]VietnamDiagnose caries stages via deep learningComparative experimentalPrimary & permanent2652 intraoral images using smartphone1 experienced dentistManual (Tool not specified)Resized/scaled to uniform resolution & Gaussian noise filteringFlipping and rotation position augmentationObject Detection & ClassificationDental caries on smooth surfacesYOLOv3, Faster R-CNN, RetinaNet, SSDSensitivity87.4
Farook et al., 2023 [33]AustraliaClassify caries from smartphone micro-imagesIn vitro simulationN/A233 micro-images from stock smartphones3 dentistsLabelImg.pyHorizontal flipping, vertical flipping, horizontal & vertical flipping, 90-degree clockwise rotation, average blurring, & hue raisingObject Detection & ClassificationDental caries lesionsYOLOv4SensitivitySpecificity9994
Salahin et al., 2023 [34]BangladeshValidate CNN-based caries detection systemExperimentalN/A233 images from a smartphone3 volunteer expertsLabelImg.pyFlipped horizontally, vertically & both ways, HSV, average blur, dropout, Hue, rotated & invertObject Detection & ClassificationSmooth surface caries lesionsYOLOv5Mean average precision (mAP)PrecisionRecall (Sensitivity)72.773.172.9
Tareq et al., 2023 [37]BangladeshPredict cavitations from non-standardized photosIn vitro simulationN/A1703 augmented images from a smartphone camera system3 dentistsNot reported13 methods of image augmentation via CLoDSA Python libraryObject Detection & ClassificationAnterior tooth smooth surface caries lesionsYOLO + ResNet, VGG16, etc.Accuracy Precision Recall86.968988
Wang et al., 2023 [43]ChinaCombine sub-band imaging and DL for diagnosisPerformance evaluationN/AHyperspectral camera for 83 specimensProfessional dentistManual (Tool not specified)Preprocessing using Otsu thresholding segmentation & Data enhancement & augmentationClassification & SegmentationDental caries & dental calculus2D−3D CNN + Efficient Channel Attention (ECA) moduleAccuracySensitivitySpecificityF1-score96.4396.0899.1196.13
Adnan et al., 2024 [35]PakistanEvaluate YOLOv5s for decay detectionDiagnostic validationPrimary & permanent7465 images captured on mobile phones2 dentistsLabelMe V5.4.1 (MIT, open source)No explicit data augmentation mentioned.Object DetectionTeeth with visible decayYOLOv5sPrecision SensitivityF1-score90.785.688
Jiang et al., 2024 [36]ChinaDevelop AI for children's caries/sealant detectionDevelopment/ testingPermanent16,023 oral images taken by cellphonesDental expertsNot reportedTiling, tile-filtering using histogramObject DetectionPermanent molars showing dental caries & PFSYOLOv5, YOLOXMean average precision (mAP)84.7
Mehta et al., 2024 [39]IndiaCompare residual vs dense networksModel comparisonN/A1064 digital RGB imagesDentistExcel-based tooth-wise labelingRescaling, shear transformation, zoom transformation, horizontal flip & sequential operation for augmented output imageClassificationIndividual teeth classified by caries severityResNet, DenseNetAccuracyF1-score9393
Boy et al., 2025 [44]IndonesiaBuild early detection model using MobileNetV3 SmallDevelopment & validationN/A1200 smartphone imagesN/AManual (Tool not specified)Resize, pixel normalization, & data augmentationClassificationIndividual teeth classified as decayed or healthyMobileNetV3 SmallAccuracyPrecisionRecall (Sensitivity)909090
Abbreviation: YOLO: You Only Look Once; SVM: Support Vector Machine; CNN: Convolutional Neural Network; N/A: Not available

Study designs included diagnostic performance evaluations, experimental studies, proof-of-concept accuracy studies, mixed-methods usability testing, in vitro simulations, and model development/validation. Eleven studies (79 %) focused on images captured using smartphone photography 32, 33, 35, 36, 37, 38, 40, 41, 42, 43, 44 while 3 studies (21 %) used real-time applications with the incorporation of AI on smartphones to detect caries 34, 39, 45. Of the 14 included studies, 6 were in vitro studies using extracted teeth or simulated environments 33, 34, 37, 40, 41, 43, and 8 were clinical studies involving patient-based data 32, 35, 36, 38, 39, 42, 44, 45. While in vitro studies are generally considered lower in the evidence hierarchy, their inclusion was essential due to the limited number of clinical studies available in this emerging field. These studies provide important early insights into the feasibility and diagnostic performance of AI models under controlled conditions.

Primary and permanent dentitions were both addressed, especially in studies focused on children or early childhood caries (ECC). Four studies (29 %) included primary and permanent dentitions, 9 studies (64 %) did not disclose the assessed dentition, and 1 study (7 %) only included the permanent dentition.

Where reported, image acquisition angles were extracted and recorded. Most studies employed frontal occlusal views, with additional right- and left-side images taken for patients with limited mouth opening [32]. Other protocols included frontal and lateral views of anterior teeth [38], anterior images under both natural and artificial lighting with consistent angles [44], and combined front views for anterior teeth with occlusal views for posterior teeth 35, 45. Occlusal views of posterior extracted teeth were also used 40, 41, 42. One study implemented a detailed protocol covering anterior and posterior regions in centric and edge-to-edge occlusion, as well as palatal/occlusal views of both arches [39]. In some cases, images were captured freehand by a single operator in a dark room to avoid ambient light interference [33]. Fluorescence imaging of dental samples was performed using different smartphones [43]. Smooth surface photography was typically taken from central, right lateral, and left lateral views [38]. Some studies excluded molars to reduce variations from shadowing, translucency changes, fissure lesions, and occlusal morphology that could impair algorithm training 34, 37. Photographs, often taken with smartphones in realistic environments, showed permanent first molars with variable image sizes (∼500 × 500 to ∼3000 × 4000 pixels), resolutions, and lighting. Some images exhibited unclear tooth surfaces or incomplete molar views due to angles or obstruction. Duplicate or unidentifiable images were excluded, though variations in size, resolution, and lighting remained [36].

The datasets used across the studies in this systematic review varied widely in terms of image source, modality, and participant demographics, illustrating the breadth of data employed for smartphone-based and AI-assisted caries detection. Several studies utilized patient-captured oral photos, such as one that generated three datasets augmented (n = 3990), enhanced (n = 3990), and a combination (n = 7980) from 570 participants, with an additional test set from 70 patients [32]. Others relied on images of extracted teeth, including 620 unrestored molars and premolars [40] and 587 images of extracted specimens [41]. Usability testing was conducted on 37 underprivileged parent-child dyads [45], while a large-scale dataset comprised 16,023 cellphone-captured oral images from children aged 7–9 [36]. Public sources also included a Kaggle dataset of 1500 human teeth images [42]. Multiple datasets were augmented, such as expanding 233 caries images into 1452 for YOLOv5 training [34], generating 1703 images from 233 extracted anterior teeth [37], and transforming 233 microphotography images into 1631 augmented versions [33].

Other notable sets included 1902 intraoral images for training and 750 for testing [38], 83 dental specimens from different individuals [43], and 7465 intraoral photos from adolescents and young adults aged 8–24 [35]. Additional datasets included 1064 intraoral images from 194 anonymized patients yielding 987 single-tooth images [39], and another featured 1200 smartphone-captured images from 50 volunteers, equally divided between decayed and healthy teeth [44]. This diverse and robust collection of datasets underpinned the training and evaluation of various AI models for caries detection across different imaging conditions and populations. Where reported, the number and qualifications of dataset annotators (e.g., general dentists, specialists, senior faculty) were extracted. The majority of studies relied on one or two expert annotators, although some employed panels of three or more clinicians. However, several studies did not provide sufficient detail on annotation protocols or reference standard validation.

sec.title: Diagnostic outcomes
sec.p:
The diagnostic outcomes of the included studies as presented in Table 4 reveals varying levels of performance across different AI models used for dental caries detection. To facilitate clearer interpretation of model capabilities, we grouped the included AI models based on their primary task type: 1) object detection models that localize caries lesions within images, 2) classification models that assign labels to entire images or regions, and 3) segmentation models that delineate lesion boundaries at the pixel level. While many models, such as YOLO and Faster R-CNN, were primarily used for detection tasks, others like ResNet or MobileNetV3 were used for classification. All of these models are based on convolutional neural networks (CNNs), and specific architectures such as MobileNetV3 or DenseNet are discussed as subtypes where relevant, without treating them as separate overarching categories.Table 4
Summary of study details according to the PICO framework.Table 4
Authors, YearParticipants (P)Intervention (I)Comparison (C)Outcome (O)
Ding et al., 2021 [32]570 patients’ mobile phone images (train), 70 patients (test)YOLOv3 with image enhancement & augmentationYOLOv3 with different preprocessing techniquesmAP improved from 56.2 % to 85.48 %. F1-score for main caries up to 0.80, secondary caries up to 0.69.
Duong et al., 2021 [40]620 smartphone images of extracted molars and premolarsSVM-based caries detection using ICDAS II classificationICDAS II visual classificationAccuracy: 92.37 %, Sensitivity: 88.1 %, Specificity: 96.6 % for cavitated caries. Lower metrics for non-cavitated caries.
Duong et al., 2021 [41]587 smartphone images of extracted teethTwo-stage AI-based system using color photosVisual ICDAS evaluationAccuracy: 87.39 %, Sensitivity: 89.88 %, Specificity: 68.86 %.
Al-Jallad et al., 2022 [45]37 parent-child dyads at high ECC riskSmartphone app for caries screening with parent-captured photosNoneFeasibility score: 78.4. SUS: 78.5 %. High user acceptability; professional involvement improves image quality.
Priya et al., 2022 [42]Kaggle dataset images of teeth with/without cariesCNNs trained on preprocessed imagesPreprocessing methodsAccuracy: 96 %, Loss: 11 %. High model performance post-training.
Thanh et al., 2022 [38]750 test photos from 250 individuals; 1902 train images from 695 individualsYOLOv3, Faster R-CNN, RetinaNet, SSDICCMS-based visual assessmentYOLOv3: Sensitivity 87.4 % for cavitated caries, 36 % for VNC; Specificity > 86 % for cavitated, > 71 % for VNC.
Farook et al., 2023 [33]233 microscope images augmented to 1631, taken via smartphone microscopesYOLOv4 using clip-on microscope imageryICDAS-based dentist assessmentSensitivity: 0.99, Specificity: 0.94. Strong correlation with expert classification.
Salahin et al., 2023 [34]233 caries images augmented to 1452YOLOv5M & YOLOv5X for caries detectionVisual classification by 3 expertsYOLOv5M: mAP = 0.727, Precision = 0.731, Recall = 0.729. YOLOv5X better for early lesion detection.
Tareq et al., 2023 [37]233 extracted anterior teeth; 1703 augmented smartphone imagesYOLOv5 variants + ResNet50, VGG16 ensemble modelsNoneFinal model (YOLOv5 + VGG16): Accuracy = 86.96 %, Precision = 0.89, Recall = 0.88. High performance using non-standard photos.
Wang et al., 2023 [43]83 dental specimensFluorescence imaging + hybrid CNN with attentionTraditional diagnostic tools & ML modelsAccuracy: 96.43; Sensitivity: 96.08;Specificity: 99.11; F1: 96.13. High accuracy and portability. Suitable for community-based use.
Adnan et al., 2024 [35]7465 intraoral images from ages 8–24YOLOv5s model in smartphone appJunior dentistsAI App: Precision = 90.7 %, Sensitivity = 85.6 %, F1 = 88.0 %. Outperformed junior dentists.
Jiang et al., 2024 [36]16,023 oral photos from children aged 7–9YOLOv5 and YOLOX via smartphoneNonemAP@0.5 = 72.3 %. Real-time detection feasible. Good for at-home use.
Mehta et al., 2024 [39]1064 intraoral images from 194 patients (age 14–70)DenseNet169/201 and ResNet50V2/101/152 for caries classificationComparison between DenseNet and ResNet modelsDenseNet201: Accuracy = 93 %. Superior performance for advanced caries.
Boy et al., 2025 [44]50 volunteers capturing dental images via smartphonesMobileNetV3 Large modelVisual/radiographic dentist assessmentsAccuracy, precision, and recall all ∼90 %. Processing time: ∼6 s/image. Highly practical for mobile deployment.

YOLOv3 was evaluated for caries detection with increasing model enhancements showing better results. An enhanced YOLOv3 model achieved a mean average precision (mAP) of 85.48 %, with 93.3 % precision, 69.4 % recall, and an F1 score of 80 % for primary caries, and 100 % precision, 52.4 % recall, and an F1 score of 69 % for secondary caries [32]. YOLOv4 achieved 99 % sensitivity and 94 % specificity, with strong correlation to dentist diagnoses [33]. YOLOv5M showed an mAP of 72.7 %, precision of 73.1 %, and recall of 72.9 %, performing well in detecting microcavitations, while YOLOv5X better identified early changes without cavitation. No single model accurately detected all types of caries [34]. An ensemble of YOLOv5 with CNNs including VGG16, improved diagnostic metrics to 86.96 % accuracy, 89 % precision, and 88 % recall, outperforming other methods with non-standard smartphone images [37]. The YOLOv5s mobile app achieved 90.7 % precision, 85.6 % sensitivity, and an F1 score of 88 %, surpassing junior dentists in performance [35]. For real-time mobile-compatible detection, YOLOv5 and YOLOX models achieved mAP.5 scores of 79.7 % and 84.7 %, respectively, supporting their use for at-home screening [36]. In a multi-phase study comparing CNN architectures, DenseNet169 and DenseNet201 consistently outperformed ResNet models, with DenseNet201 achieving 93 % accuracy, 93 % recall, and 93 % F1-score, especially effective in detecting advanced caries [39].

Four deep learning models, YOLOv3, Faster R-CNN, RetinaNet, and SSD, were tested in one study for caries detection. YOLOv3 had the highest sensitivity for cavitated caries (87.4 %), followed by Faster R-CNN (71.4 %). However, both performed poorly for visually non-cavitated (VNC) lesions, with sensitivities of 36.9 % and 26 %, respectively. Specificity was high for all models over 86 % for cavitated and 71 % for VNC lesions [38]. Support Vector Machine (SVM) was also used and achieved high performance in detecting cavitated vs. other lesions, with 92.3 % accuracy, 88.1 % sensitivity, and 96.6 % specificity. Performance dropped when distinguishing VNC from non-sound conditions, with lower specificity (66.7 %) [40]. Additionally, deep convolutional neural networks including ResNet-18, ResNet-50, and GoogleNet were evaluated in another study. These models, when assessed collectively, yielded an accuracy of 87.39 %, sensitivity of 89.88 %, and specificity of 68.86 % based on comparison to visual assessments using modified ICDAS criteria [41]. Similarly, a 2D-3D Hybrid Convolutional Neural Network with Efficient Channel Attention (ECA) achieved excellent diagnostic performance for caries detection, with 96.43 % accuracy, 96.08 % sensitivity, 99.11 % specificity, and an F1 score of 96.13 %. The model was noted for being accurate, affordable, and portable-suitable for use in community and home settings [43]. Another study conducted a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), which achieved 96 % accuracy with a low loss value of 11 %, demonstrating strong performance in both training and validation stages [42]. One study evaluated MobileNetV3 Small, which achieved 90 % accuracy, precision, and recall despite variations in image quality. The model processed each image in about 6 s, highlighting its speed and practicality for field applications [44].

AI image recognition was also found feasible for parent-taken photos, with a System Usability Scale (SUS) score of 78.4 %, though image quality could be improved with the involvement of community health workers [45].

sec.title: Assessment of methodological quality
sec.p:
Detailed information on the risk of bias and applicability concerns is presented in Table 5 and Fig. 3. Using the QUADAS-2 tool, five studies were identified as having low risk of bias in all four domains 33, 35, 37, 38, 45, while eight studies demonstrated low applicability concerns in all three domains 33, 35, 36, 37, 38, 41, 44, 45.Table 5
Risk of bias and applicability concerns of included studies.Table 5
StudyRisk of biasApplicability concerns
Patient selectionIndex testReference standardFlow and timingPatient selectionIndex testReference standard
Ding et al., 2021 [32]UnclearLowUnclearUnclearUnclearLowUnclear
Duong et al., 2021 [40]UnclearLowLowLowUnclearLowLow
Duong et al., 2021 [41]UnclearLowLowLowLowLowLow
Al-Jallad et al., 2022 [45]LowLowLowLowLowLowLow
Priya et al., 2022 [42]UnclearLowUnclearUnclearUnclearLowUnclear
Thanh et al., 2022 [38]LowLowLowLowLowLowLow
Farook et al., 2023 [33]LowLowLowLowLowLowLow
Salahin et al., 2023 [34]UnclearLowUnclearUnclearUnclearLowLow
Tareq et al., 2023 [37]LowLowLowLowLowLowLow
Wang et al., 2023 [43]LowLowUnclearLowLowLowUnclear
Adnan et al., 2024 [35]LowLowLowLowLowLowLow
Jiang et al., 2024 [36]LowLowLowUnclearLowLowLow
Mehta et al., 2024 [39]UnclearLowUnclearUnclearUnclearLowUnclear
Boy et al., 2025 [44]UnclearLowLowLowLowLowLowFig. 3
Risk of bias assessment using quality assessment tool for diagnostic accuracy studies (QUADAS-2).Fig. 3

In the domain of patient selection, seven studies were assessed as having a low risk of bias, whereas the remaining seven were categorized as having an unclear risk. All included studies were judged to have a low risk of bias in the index test domain. For the reference standard, nine studies showed a low risk of bias, and five had an unclear risk. Regarding the flow and timing domain, nine studies were evaluated as low risk and five as having an unclear risk.

In terms of applicability concerns, patient selection was deemed to have low concern in nine studies, while five were classified as unclear. All studies were considered to have low applicability concerns for the index test. For the reference standard, ten studies were judged to have low concern, and four were categorized as having unclear applicability. In addition, the implications for clinical practice for each of the discussed works are shown in detail in Table 6.Table 6
Clinical implication for clinical practice of systematic review.Table 6
Authors, YearImplications for clinical practice
Ding et al., 2021 [32]Deep learning with smartphone photos enhances early caries detection and addresses public health disparities.
Duong et al., 2021 [40]An AI-enabled smartphone app can support early detection of oral health issues, facilitate timely dental care, and assist dentists in making faster, more accurate diagnoses.
Duong et al., 2021 [41]Approach is easy to use, low-cost, non-invasive, and suitable for public health research with minimal computational needs.
Al-Jallad et al., 2022 [45]Supports future clinical trials for early ECC detection and prevention in low-income children using smartphone-based AI.
Priya et al., 2022 [42]Allows users to analyze tooth images for cavities with high diagnostic performance, promoting practical clinical application.
Thanh et al., 2022 [38]Demonstrates performance of DL models (Faster R-CNN, YOLOv3, etc.) in detecting caries. Enhancing image quality and algorithm training can improve real-world applicability.
Farook et al., 2023 [33]Smartphone-based AI enhances remote caries screening, teleconsultation, and referral systems. Supports teledentistry integration.
Salahin et al., 2023 [34]Validates AI’s use of smartphone images for accurate caries detection. Aids remote dental screening and quality standardization in underserved areas.
Tareq et al., 2023 [37]AI systems using ensemble and transfer learning yield accurate cavity detection in non-standard images; valuable for teledentistry and remote diagnostics.
Wang et al., 2023 [43]Offers a portable, low-cost AI tool for early caries detection in community or at-home settings; comparable to hyperspectral imaging.
Adnan et al., 2024 [35]YOLOv5s-based app supports dental surveillance in rural areas, mapping disease, and aiding DMFT scoring; clinical diagnosis still requires dentist validation.
Jiang et al., 2024 [36]Low-complexity AI system effective for home-based screening and planning preventive care (e.g., PFS) for children.
Mehta et al., 2024 [39]AI-based mobile tools enhance patient education and assist practitioners in caries severity assessment during exams.
Boy et al., 2025 [44]Promotes early caries detection through accessible AI apps; encourages interdisciplinary collaboration to improve public health outcomes.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The primary objective of this systematic review was to synthesize current evidence on the diagnostic performance, clinical feasibility, and implementation potential of AI-driven smartphone imaging technologies for dental caries detection. Considering the persistent global burden of untreated dental caries especially in low-resource and underserved settings, AI-enabled smartphone tools represent a transformative solution to bridge diagnostic gaps by offering accessible, scalable, and cost-effective detection strategies.

sec.title: Key findings
sec.p: The diagnostic accuracy of AI-based smartphone imaging tools for caries detection, when compared to conventional diagnostic modalities, has demonstrated promising results across multiple studies. A wide range of AI platforms, including convolutional neural networks (CNNs), YOLO variants, ResNet, and MobileNet, were evaluated with metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, and F1-score.

sec.title: Diagnostic accuracy of AI-driven smartphone imaging
sec.p: Multiple studies reported high diagnostic accuracy across a range of AI models. YOLO-based object detection models consistently reported strong performance, with enhanced YOLOv3 achieving a mean average precision (mAP) of 85.5 % and an F1-score of 0.80 for primary caries detection [32]. Similarly, support vector machine (SVM) and ResNet models demonstrated accuracies of 92.3 % and 87.39 %, respectively, with specificity exceeding 96.6 % in one of the SVM configurations [40]. YOLOv4 achieved 99 % sensitivity and 94 % specificity aligned with ICDAS reference standards [33]. Several object detection models were compared, with YOLOv3 and Faster R-CNN achieving high sensitivity for cavitated caries, 87.4 % and 71.4 %, respectively, though their performance declined for non-cavitated lesions [38]. YOLOv5 models demonstrated robust detection capabilities for early lesions, with YOLOv5M and YOLOv5X attaining mAP values of 72.7 % and precision and recall exceeding 70 % [34]. In comparative studies, YOLOv5s outperformed junior dentists with 90.7 % precision and an 88 % F1-score [35]. A CNN model achieved 96 % accuracy and a low loss value of 11 %, indicating robust validation performance [42].

Other architectures also achieved high diagnostic performance. DenseNet201 reported 93 % accuracy and F1-score, outperforming several ResNet variants [39], while MobileNetV3 Small achieved 90 % accuracy, precision, and recall, making it attractive for real-time use [44]. Hybrid and ensemble approaches further improved diagnostic performance, such as a 2D–3D CNN reaching 96.4 % accuracy and 99.1 % specificity [43], and VGG16-enhanced models outperforming standard YOLOv5 implementations with 86.96 % accuracy, 89 % precision, and 88 % recall, [37]. However, diagnostic performance was not uniform. Most models showed reduced sensitivity for non-cavitated or early lesions, and results varied across datasets due to differences in imaging angles, lighting, and preprocessing methods 34, 38, 42.

sec.title: Feasibility and usability in clinical and public health settings
sec.p: The reviewed studies consistently highlight the feasibility of AI-based smartphone applications for caries detection in clinical, community, and home settings. Several studies reported successful deployment of mobile-compatible models, such as YOLOv5 and YOLOX, which achieved mAP values of 79.7 % and 84.7 % with minimal latency under real-world conditions [36]. A hybrid 2D–3D CNN model also showed exceptional diagnostic performance (96.43 % accuracy, 99.11 % specificity) while remaining affordable and suitable for community-level use [43].

User-centered evaluations demonstrated high acceptability, with one study reporting a System Usability Scale (SUS) score of 78.4, indicating excellent usability [45]. These applications were valued for their ease of use, speed, and responsiveness. They also enabled non-dental personnel, such as community health workers and caregivers, to capture diagnostic-quality images and perform preliminary screenings. Although diagnostic accuracy was slightly lower without professional oversight, results remained clinically meaningful for identifying cavitated lesions and facilitating referrals 36, 40, 43.

Another AI platform, the EfficientDet-Lite1 neural network, was trained using radiographic images and deployed as an AI app on a Google Pixel 6 smartphone. Using the phone’s rear camera, the app detected caries lesions in real time on bitewing radiographs (BWRs), achieving sensitivity and precision scores ranging from 57.5 % to 75 % and 69.2–84.6 %, respectively 24, 25. Two scanning methods, moving the phone over static BWRs and keeping the phone stationary while scanning moving BWRs, were evaluated. These proof-of-concept studies highlight the potential of AI-powered smartphone apps as fast, accessible, and user-friendly tools for caries diagnosis. However, these studies were excluded from the systematic review due to their reliance on radiographic images, despite their integration of AI with smartphone technology 24, 25.

AI-powered smartphone tools also enhance clinical workflows by supporting early detection, improving diagnostic accuracy, and facilitating patient communication 39, 40. They have demonstrated utility in early childhood caries (ECC) detection 41, 45, and could strengthen teledentistry services by enabling remote screening, diagnosis, and referral 33, 40. Their affordability, portability, and non-invasive nature make them especially suitable for underserved areas, where they can support large-scale oral health screening and surveillance initiatives 36, 37, 41, 43.

sec.title: Barriers and facilitators for integration into routine dental practice
sec.p: While the promise of AI in dentistry is compelling, its widespread adoption is contingent on overcoming a range of technical, ethical, regulatory, and behavioral challenges. In reviewing the current literature on AI-based dental caries detection using mobile or smartphone-compatible applications, several barriers and facilitators were consistently observed across studies. A prominent limitation noted by multiple authors was the restricted and often imbalanced datasets, which affected the generalizability and robustness of AI models 32, 34, 38, 39, 40, 42. The collection and annotation of high-quality dental images require significant time and resources [32] and many datasets were derived from limited geographical locations or specific populations, such as rural communities or specific age groups, which further constrained model applicability 35, 45. Moreover, many studies used in vitro images or those sourced from the internet, which lacked clinical validation and histological confirmation 34, 41. Other common image-related challenges included poor resolution, variations in lighting and angle, and the presence of artifacts such as plaque or annotations, all of which impeded accurate lesion detection 36, 38, 44.

Several technical and operational constraints were also reported. High computational demands for training advanced models like DenseNet201, ANNs limited their usability on low-resource devices 37, 39. Moreover, some models exhibited latency or reduced inference speed on smartphones [36], while others could not yet perform real-time detection or determine lesion severity 40, 41, 42. Additionally, many studies did not validate their AI outputs against gold-standard clinical diagnoses or lacked multi-center trials, limiting the reliability and external validity of their findings 33, 39, 45. Despite these limitations, the reviewed studies demonstrated notable facilitators that support the feasibility and potential of AI-based mobile caries detection. The use of preprocessing techniques such as image augmentation, resizing, and tiling improved model robustness and feature detection under varied lighting conditions 36, 42. Some studies successfully deployed their AI models into mobile applications, showing strong usability and diagnostic potential for home-based or remote caries screening, especially in underserved populations 35, 45. Several studies in this review reported that specific image enhancement techniques such as contrast normalization, brightness correction, cropping, and resizing, were associated with improved model performance. These enhancements likely helped reduce background noise and standardize input images for better feature detection by AI models. However, aggressive image enhancements risk introducing artifacts that obscure diagnostic features, emphasizing the need for careful selection and validation of preprocessing methods.

Additional strategies such as transfer learning, explainable AI (XAI), and integration with smartphone-specific features (e.g., enhanced cameras, automated segmentation) improve interpretability, scalability, and user trust 33, 35, 36. Several authors highlighted the promise of community-level deployment and teledentistry integration, noting that AI-powered tools could empower non-dental health workers and increase access to dental care in low-resource or rural settings 34, 35, 44. Ongoing and future work also includes efforts to standardize image acquisition, expand datasets through multi-center collaboration, and incorporate additional diagnostic features such as behavioral or lifestyle data to enhance overall diagnostic accuracy and clinical relevance 32, 40, 43.

sec.title: Future directions and emerging architectures
sec.p: While current AI models have shown remarkable promise in detecting dental caries using smartphone-based imaging, the rapid evolution of deep learning continues to open new avenues for innovation. Recent advancements highlight the potential for even greater diagnostic accuracy, efficiency, and clinical utility, particularly as emerging architectures push the boundaries of what AI can achieve in dental diagnostics.

Among these, YOLOv12, Masked Deep Embedding of Patches (MDEP), and Vision Transformer (ViT)-based approaches represent significant leaps forward. The MDEP pre-training method, for instance, has demonstrated notable improvements in caries detection on dental radiographs by leveraging self-supervised learning, which enables robust performance even with limited training data [46]. Similarly, the Segment Anything Model for Medical Imaging (MedSAM) [47] offers exceptional feature extraction and real-time segmentation capabilities, suggesting that such models could dramatically enhance lesion detection and annotation in dental imaging, although they remain largely underexplored in this field. YOLOv12, in particular, has shown outstanding performance in detecting caries from color images, significantly outperforming earlier methods such as Multi-Step Segmentation with K-Means (MSS-KM). It achieved superior accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and mean average precision (mAP), underscoring its effectiveness, reliability, and robustness across different experimental conditions [48]. These findings emphasize the transformative potential of next-generation architectures, which not only improve diagnostic performance but also offer scalability and adaptability for real-world dental applications. Future research should aim to systematically benchmark these new models against established architectures to evaluate their performance in detecting both cavitated and non-cavitated lesions under diverse clinical conditions.

Multimodal deep learning, integrating clinical images, patient history, and behavioral data, can improve diagnostic accuracy and personalized care [49]. Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative training without sharing raw data, addressing privacy and data scarcity challenges [50]. In parallel, adopting explainable AI (XAI) will be essential to build clinician trust, enhance model transparency, and support regulatory approval [51]. Finally, integrating AI with augmented reality (AR) and teledentistry platforms offers exciting possibilities, such as real-time lesion annotation, remote diagnosis, and interactive patient education. Realizing the full potential of these technologies will require close collaboration among dental professionals, computer scientists, and policymakers to ensure clinical relevance, ethical standards, and equitable access.

sec.title: Study limitations
sec.p: This systematic review has several limitations that should be considered when interpreting the findings. First, significant heterogeneity was observed across the included studies regarding study design, image acquisition protocols, datasets, and reference standards. Many models were trained on small, geographically limited, or demographically specific datasets, limiting their generalizability. Additionally, the frequent use of in vitro or publicly available datasets without clinical validation reduces real-world applicability. Second, variations in imaging angles, lighting conditions, and annotation practices introduce bias and complicate cross-study comparisons. Most studies did not specify whether they targeted primary or permanent dentitions, reducing the applicability of findings across age groups. Furthermore, few studies reported inter-annotator agreement or used consensus-based labeling, raising concerns about annotation reliability and potential diagnostic bias. Third, only a limited number of studies evaluated usability or feasibility in real-world clinical settings, leaving gaps in understanding the practical implementation challenges. Moreover, most AI models were not externally validated against gold-standard diagnostic benchmarks or across multiple clinical sites.

Future studies should therefore prioritize standardized study designs, consistent imaging protocols, and clinically diverse datasets. Incorporating consensus-based annotation, expanding data through multi-center collaborations, and adopting open-access, privacy-preserving datasets will improve reproducibility and robustness. Finally, real-world validation especially in low-resource and community settings, will be crucial to ensuring the clinical readiness of AI-driven smartphone tools for caries detection.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This systematic review highlights the strong potential of AI-driven smartphone imaging as an accessible and cost-effective tool for dental caries detection. Current evidence demonstrates that deep learning models, particularly YOLOv5, DenseNet, and MobileNetV3, achieve high diagnostic accuracy and usability. However, diagnostic sensitivity for early lesions remains limited, and challenges around data quality, standardization, and clinical validation persist.

To remain aligned with state-of-the-art AI, future studies should evaluate newer architectures such as YOLOv12, Vision Transformers, and MedSAM, which have shown superior performance in medical imaging tasks. Integrating clinician input, standardized datasets, and explainable AI approaches will be critical to building trust and ensuring reliable clinical adoption. Ultimately, AI-powered smartphone imaging has the potential to transform both clinical dentistry and community-based oral health programs, particularly in underserved and low-resource settings.

sec.title: Declaration of Generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
sec.p: During the preparation of this work, the authors used ChatGPT to refine language, improve clarity, and correct spelling and grammatical errors. After using this tool, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and take full responsibility for the content of the publication.

sec.title: Declaration of Competing Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflict of interest.
